steps.title: Wat Doe Je Graag?
steps.desc: Before making plans, you need hobbies. Dutch uses 'graag' (gladly) where English uses 'like to': 'Ik zwem graag' = I like to swim. This lesson teaches hobby/leisure vocabulary and the graag construction.

steps.example: Ik zwem graag.
steps.exampleEn: I like to swim.
steps.note:
Regular verb. Stam = zwem.
'Graag' goes after the verb for 'like to':
Ik zwem graag = I gladly swim = I like swimming.

steps.example: Zij leest graag boeken.
steps.exampleEn: She likes reading books.
steps.note:
Irregular in past tense (gelezen), but regular in present.
Stam = lees. zij leest (stam + t).

steps.example: Wij koken graag samen.
steps.exampleEn: We like cooking together.
steps.note:
Regular. Stam = kook.
You already know this from Unit 4!

steps.example: Ik wandel graag in het bos.
steps.exampleEn: I like walking in the forest.
steps.note:
The 'recreational walk' verb from Unit 7.
'Het bos' = the forest. Popular Dutch weekend activity.

steps.example: Hij sport drie keer per week.
steps.exampleEn: He exercises three times a week.
steps.note:
Verb form of 'sport'. Very common.
'Sporten' covers gym, running, cycling, etc.
The Dutch are among Europe's most active people.

steps.example: Wij wandelen in het bos.
steps.exampleEn: We walk in the forest.
steps.note:
Het-word. The Netherlands has more forests than you'd think.
Veluwe, Utrechtse Heuvelrug, Hoge Veluwe national park.

steps.title: 📐 Graag vs Liever vs Het Liefst
steps.text:
GRAAG = gladly (= like to)
Ik zwem graag. = I like swimming.

LIEVER = rather (= prefer to)
Ik fiets liever. = I prefer cycling.

HET LIEFST = most of all (= like most)
Ik wandel het liefst. = I like walking most of all.

This is a comparative system:
graag → liever → het liefst
(gladly → rather → most gladly)

Example conversation:
Wat doe je graag? = What do you like doing?
Ik zwem graag, maar ik fiets liever.
= I like swimming, but I prefer cycling.
Ik wandel het liefst.
= What I like most is walking.

steps.s: Ik fiets ___ dan wandelen.
Ik zwem {1}, maar ik wandel {2}.
steps.hint: I like swimming, but I prefer walking.

steps.s: Wat doe je ___? (= What do you like doing?)

steps.title: Ik Ga Zwemmen
steps.desc: Dutch uses 'gaan + infinitive' for the near future — identical to English 'I'm going to swim.' This is the most common way to talk about plans in Dutch. Simple, natural, and you already know 'gaan'.

steps.example: Ik ga morgen zwemmen.
steps.exampleEn: I'm going to swim tomorrow.
steps.note:
gaan + infinitive = going to [verb].
Exact same structure as English!
The infinitive goes to the END (like modals).

steps.example: Volgende week ga ik op vakantie.
steps.exampleEn: Next week I'm going on holiday.
steps.note:
volgende = next/following.
Volgende week, volgende maand, volgend jaar.
Note: 'volgend jaar' (het-word → no -e).

steps.example: Ik ga binnenkort naar de dokter.
steps.exampleEn: I'm going to the doctor soon.
steps.note:
binnen + kort = within + short.
Literally: 'within a short time'.
'Binnenkort' = soon but not immediate.

steps.example: Wij gaan volgende week op vakantie.
steps.exampleEn: We're going on holiday next week.
steps.note:
'Op vakantie gaan' = to go on holiday.
The Dutch LOVE holidays. Average: 25 vacation days/year.
'Waar ga je op vakantie?' = Where are you going on holiday?

steps.example: Wij gaan samen eten.
steps.exampleEn: We're going to eat together.
steps.note:
Very common word.
'Samen' makes everything warmer.
'Zullen we samen gaan?' = Shall we go together?

steps.title: 📐 Future with 'gaan'
steps.text:
Subject + gaan + ... + infinitive (at end)

Ik ga morgen zwemmen. = I'm going to swim tomorrow.
Wij gaan volgende week op vakantie.
= We're going on holiday next week.
Zij gaat vanavond koken.
= She's going to cook tonight.

This is the casual/spoken future.
Dutch also has 'zullen' (shall/will) — next lesson.
But 'gaan + infinitive' covers 90% of future plans.

Negation: Ik ga morgen niet zwemmen.
= I'm not going to swim tomorrow.
'Niet' goes before the infinitive.

Question: Ga je morgen zwemmen?
= Are you going to swim tomorrow?

steps.s: Wij ___ volgende week op vakantie.
steps.hint: We ARE GOING on holiday (wij = gaan)

steps.s: Zij {1} vanavond {2}.
steps.hint: She IS GOING TO cook tonight.

steps.s: ___ je morgen zwemmen?
steps.hint: Are you going to swim? (question: -t drops)
Same rule as modals and perfect tense

steps.title: Zal Ik...?
steps.desc: The second future construction: 'zullen' (shall/will). Less common than 'gaan' for plans, but essential for offers ('Shall I help?'), promises ('I will do it'), and predictions. Plus: making suggestions in Dutch.
goals: Zullen vs gaan: when to use which

steps.example: Zal ik je helpen?
steps.exampleEn: Shall I help you?
steps.note:
Modal-like verb. Same word order.
ik zal, jij zult/zal, hij zal, wij zullen.
More formal/distant than 'gaan'.

steps.example: Zal ik koffie maken?
steps.exampleEn: Shall I make coffee?
steps.note:
THE offer construction.
'Zal ik [infinitive]?' = Shall I [verb]?
Very polite and helpful-sounding.

steps.example: Ik zal het doen.
steps.exampleEn: I will do it.
steps.note:
Promise/intention.
'Ik zal het niet vergeten.' = I won't forget it.
More formal than 'ik ga het doen'.

steps.example: Kan ik je helpen?
steps.exampleEn: Can I help you?
steps.note:
Regular in present. Irregular past: geholpen.
Identical to English. 'Help!' = 'Help!'

steps.example: Ik zal het niet vergeten.
steps.exampleEn: I won't forget it.
steps.note:
ver- prefix → no ge- in past participle: vergeten.
The past participle is the same as the infinitive!
vergeten → vergeten. (Like English: forget → forgotten)

steps.title: ⚖️ Gaan vs Zullen
steps.text:
GAAN = concrete plans (going to)
Ik ga morgen zwemmen. = I'm going to swim tomorrow.
(You've decided. It's planned.)

ZULLEN = offers, promises, predictions
Zal ik helpen? = Shall I help? (offer)
Ik zal het doen. = I will do it. (promise)
Het zal morgen regenen. = It will rain tomorrow. (prediction)

In practice:
'Gaan' covers 90% of future talk.
'Zullen' is for offers ('Zal ik...?') and promises.

Dutch people rarely say 'ik zal morgen zwemmen.'
They say 'ik ga morgen zwemmen.'
'Zullen' sounds more formal/distant for plans.

steps.hint: Shall I...? = offering to do something

steps.s: Ik ___ het niet vergeten.
{1} ik je helpen? Ik {2} morgen zwemmen.
steps.hint: Shall I help? (offer) I'm going to swim. (plan)

steps.s: ___ ik koffie maken?

steps.title: Omdat Het Leuk Is
steps.desc: The hardest word-order rule in Dutch: subordinate clauses. Since Unit 3 you've been using V2 — the verb always in position 2. Now meet V-FINAL: when you use 'omdat' (because), 'dat' (that), or 'als' (if/when), the verb goes to the END. This is the #1 error even at B1 level. We're tackling it head-on.
goals: Why this exists and how to practice

steps.title: 🔑 The Verb Goes to the End
steps.text:
Remember the V2 rule from Unit 3?
In main clauses, the verb is ALWAYS in position 2:
Ik drink koffie. = I drink coffee.
Soms drink ik koffie. = Sometimes I drink coffee.

Subordinate clauses break this rule.
The verb moves to the VERY END:
...omdat ik koffie DRINK. = ...because I drink coffee.

Main: Het is leuk.
Subordinate: ...omdat het leuk IS.

Main: Ik kan niet komen.
Subordinate: ...omdat ik niet kan KOMEN.
       (OR: ...omdat ik niet KAN komen.)

Trigger words (conjunctions):
omdat = because
dat = that
als = if / when
wanneer = when
terwijl = while
hoewel = although

After ANY of these, the conjugated verb moves to the end.
This is the #1 Dutch word order rule and the #1 error.
Practice it until it becomes automatic.

steps.example: Ik leer Nederlands omdat ik in Amsterdam woon.
steps.exampleEn: I'm learning Dutch because I live in Amsterdam.
steps.note:
The most common subordinating conjunction.
After 'omdat': VERB TO THE END.
...omdat ik in Amsterdam WOON.
Not: ...omdat ik woon in Amsterdam.

steps.example: Ik denk dat hij ziek is.
steps.exampleEn: I think that he is sick.
steps.note:
'That' as conjunction (not demonstrative).
After 'dat': verb to the end.
...dat hij ziek IS.
Not: ...dat hij is ziek.

steps.example: Als het regent, blijf ik thuis.
steps.exampleEn: If it rains, I stay home.
steps.note:
Two meanings: 'if' and 'when' (conditions).
After 'als': verb to the end.
...als het REGENT...
Not: ...als regent het...

steps.example: Ik denk dat het gaat regenen.
steps.exampleEn: I think that it's going to rain.
steps.note:
Regular verb. Stam = denk.
Often followed by 'dat': 'Ik denk dat...'

steps.example: Ik weet dat hij in Rotterdam woont.
steps.exampleEn: I know that he lives in Rotterdam.
steps.note:
Irregular: ik weet, jij weet, wij weten.
Distinct from 'kennen' (to know a person).
'Ik weet het niet.' = I don't know.
English 'wit' used to mean 'to know' — as in
'to wit' (= namely), 'witness' (one who knows),
and 'witty' (originally: knowledgeable, clever).
Dutch kept 'weten' as the everyday word for knowing facts.

steps.example: Het regent, dus ik blijf thuis.
steps.exampleEn: It's raining, so I stay home.
steps.note:
Coordinating conjunction — NORMAL word order.
Compare:
omdat → verb goes to END: ...omdat het regent.
dus → verb stays in position 2: ...dus ik blijf thuis.

'Dus' is English 'thus' — same Germanic root.

steps.title: ⚠️ With Modal Verbs
steps.text:
When a subordinate clause has a modal verb,
BOTH verbs go to the end:

Main: Ik kan niet komen.
Subordinate: ...omdat ik niet kan komen.
             OR: ...omdat ik niet komen kan.

Both orders are accepted in modern Dutch.
The most natural: modal + infinitive at the end.

...omdat ik niet KAN KOMEN.
...dat ik morgen MOET WERKEN.
...als ik niet KAN ZWEMMEN.

With perfect tense:
...omdat ik heb GEWERKT.
OR: ...omdat ik GEWERKT heb.

Again, both are fine. Don't overthink it.
Just get the verb(s) to the end.

steps.q: '...omdat ik in Amsterdam woon.' — the verb is at the END because:

steps.s: Ik leer Nederlands omdat ik in Nederland ___.
steps.hint: ...because I LIVE in the Netherlands (verb at end)
dat hij ziek IS — verb at end

steps.s: Ik blijf thuis omdat het {1}. Ik denk dat hij {2} is.
Als het mooi weer ___, ga ik fietsen.
steps.hint: If the weather nice IS... (verb at end in als-clause)

steps.q: In subordinate clauses, Dutch word order becomes:

steps.s: Ik weet dat hij in Rotterdam ___.
steps.hint: ...that he in Rotterdam LIVES (verb at end, hij = -t)

steps.title: Als Het Mooi Weer Is...
steps.desc: Practice subordinate clauses with 'als' (if/when) in real scenarios: weather, plans, conditions. Also introduces 'wanneer' (when) and the important structure where the subordinate clause comes FIRST.
goals: Subordinate clause FIRST → inverted main clause

steps.example: Het weer is mooi vandaag.
steps.exampleEn: The weather is nice today.
steps.note:
Het-word. THE Dutch small-talk topic.
The Netherlands has unpredictable weather.
'Lekker weer!' = Nice weather!

steps.example: Het regent vaak in Nederland.
steps.exampleEn: It rains often in the Netherlands.
steps.note:
'Het regent' = it rains. Impersonal verb.
Rain is the defining Dutch weather experience.
'Wij fietsen in de regen.' = We cycle in the rain.

steps.example: Ik fiets door de regen.
steps.exampleEn: I cycle through the rain.
steps.note:
De-word. 'In de regen' = in the rain.
The Dutch don't cancel plans because of rain.
They just cycle faster.

steps.example: De zon schijnt!
steps.exampleEn: The sun is shining!
steps.note:
De-word. 'De zon schijnt' = the sun shines.
When the sun appears in NL, everything stops.
Terraces fill. Parks fill. Life moves outside.

steps.example: Wanneer het zomer is, ga ik zwemmen.
steps.exampleEn: When it's summer, I go swimming.
steps.note:
'Wanneer' = when (subordinating conjunction).
Same verb-final rule as 'als'.
Subtle difference: wanneer = more specific time.

steps.title: 📐 Subordinate Clause First
steps.text:
When the subordinate clause comes FIRST,
the main clause starts with the verb (inversion):

Normal: Ik ga fietsen als het mooi weer is.
= I'm going cycling if the weather is nice.

Reversed: Als het mooi weer is, ga ik fietsen.
= If the weather is nice, I'm going cycling.

The comma separates the clauses.
After the comma: VERB first, then subject.

More examples:
Omdat het regent, blijf ik thuis.
= Because it's raining, I stay home.

Als ik tijd heb, ga ik wandelen.
= If I have time, I'll go walking.

This looks complex but the rule is simple:
Subordinate clause = verb at end.
Main clause after subordinate = verb first.

steps.q: 'Als het regent, blijf ik thuis.' — why 'blijf' before 'ik'?
opts: The subordinate clause came first → main clause inverts
steps.ans: The subordinate clause came first → main clause inverts
steps.hint: Subordinate first → verb-subject in main clause

steps.s: Als het mooi weer ___, ga ik fietsen.
steps.hint: If the weather nice IS (verb at end)

steps.s: Als het {1}, blijf ik thuis. Als de zon {2}, ga ik fietsen.
steps.hint: If it rains... If the sun shines...

steps.s: ___ ik tijd heb, ga ik wandelen.

steps.q: The Dutch attitude to rain is:

steps.title: Om Te...
steps.desc: 'Om... te...' = 'in order to.' A purpose clause that sends the infinitive to the end. 'Ik leer Nederlands om in Amsterdam te werken.' Clean, elegant, and very common in Dutch daily speech.

steps.example: Ik leer Nederlands om in Amsterdam te werken.
steps.exampleEn: I'm learning Dutch in order to work in Amsterdam.
steps.note:
'Om' starts the purpose clause.
'Te' goes right before the infinitive.
The infinitive is at the END.
om [middle stuff] te [infinitive].

steps.example: Het is niet nodig om te betalen.
steps.exampleEn: It's not necessary to pay.
steps.note:
Adjective. 'Nodig hebben' = to need.
'Ik heb een pen nodig.' = I need a pen.
'Het is nodig om...' = It is necessary to...

steps.example: Ik probeer om Nederlands te spreken.
steps.exampleEn: I try to speak Dutch.
steps.note:
Regular verb. Stam = probeer.
'Proberen om... te...' = try to...
'Probeer het maar!' = Just try it!

steps.title: 📐 Om Te in Practice
steps.text:
Purpose:
Ik leer Nederlands om in Amsterdam te werken.
= I'm learning Dutch in order to work in Amsterdam.

Ik fiets om gezond te blijven.
= I cycle in order to stay healthy.

With separable verbs (ge-/prefix goes back together):
Ik bel om een afspraak te maken.
= I'm calling to make an appointment.
(NOT: om een afspraak te 'maak')

After certain verbs:
Ik probeer om op tijd te komen.
= I try to come on time.

Hij vergeet om te betalen.
= He forgets to pay.

Note: some verbs use 'te' without 'om':
Ik probeer te slapen. = I try to sleep.
But with 'om' is also correct and very common.

steps.q: 'Ik leer Nederlands om in Amsterdam te werken.' — 'om...te' expresses:

steps.s: Ik fiets ___ gezond te blijven.
steps.hint: I cycle IN ORDER TO stay healthy

steps.s: Ik leer Nederlands {1} in Amsterdam te {2}.
steps.hint: I learn Dutch IN ORDER TO work in Amsterdam.

steps.s: Ik ___ om Nederlands te spreken.
steps.hint: I TRY to speak Dutch (ik = stam)

steps.title: Koningsdag!
steps.desc: April 27: the entire Netherlands turns orange. Koningsdag (King's Day) is the biggest national celebration — bigger than Christmas, bigger than New Year. Free markets, live music, boats, beer, and national pride. This is where language meets culture at full volume.
goals: Practice all Unit 9 grammar in context

steps.title: 🇳🇱 Koningsdag: April 27
steps.text:
Koningsdag (King's Day) = King Willem-Alexander's birthday.

The rules:
• Wear orange. Everything orange. No exceptions.
• The whole country becomes a vrijmarkt (free market).
  Anyone can sell anything on the street. No permits needed.
• Live music on every corner.
• Amsterdam's canals fill with boats blasting music.
• Beer. Lots of beer. Starting at 8 AM.

'Oranje gekte' = orange madness.

Koningsnacht (King's Night, April 26) is when it starts.
By noon on April 27, every street is packed.

The color orange? From the House of Orange-Nassau,
the Dutch royal family. William of Orange (1533–1584)
led the Dutch revolt against Spain.

'Lang leve de Koning!' = Long live the King!
'Oranje boven!' = Orange on top! (national cheer)

steps.example: De koning woont in Den Haag.
steps.exampleEn: The king lives in The Hague.
steps.note:
De-word. Current king: Willem-Alexander (since 2013).
The Dutch monarchy is very popular.
The royal family is seen as 'normaal' — the ultimate Dutch compliment.

steps.example: Wij gaan Koningsdag vieren!
steps.exampleEn: We're going to celebrate King's Day!
steps.note:
Regular verb. Stam = vier.
'Vieren' works for all celebrations:
verjaardag vieren, Koningsdag vieren.

steps.example: Iedereen draagt oranje!
steps.exampleEn: Everyone wears orange!
steps.note:
Both the color and the national symbol.
'Oranje' = the royal House of Orange.
On Koningsdag: orange clothes, orange food, orange everything.

steps.example: Koningsdag is het grootste feest van het jaar.
steps.exampleEn: King's Day is the biggest party of the year.
steps.note:
Het-word. 'Een feestje' = a little party (diminutive).
'Feesten' = to party (verb). Cognate of English 'feast'.

steps.example: Op de vrijmarkt kun je alles kopen.
steps.exampleEn: At the free market you can buy everything.
steps.note:
vrij + markt = free + market.
Anyone can sell anything. Kids sell old toys.
Adults sell second-hand clothes. No rules!

steps.s: Iedereen draagt ___ op Koningsdag.

steps.hint: William of Orange, the founding father

steps.s: Wij {1} Koningsdag vieren! Het is het grootste {2} van het jaar.
steps.hint: We're GOING TO celebrate! The biggest PARTY.

steps.s: Op de ___ kun je alles kopen en verkopen.

steps.title: Unit 9: Alles Samen
steps.desc: Future with gaan and zullen. Subordinate clauses with omdat/dat/als. Purpose clauses with om...te. Hobbies, weather, and Koningsdag. All of Unit 9 mixed together.

steps.title: 📋 What You Know Now
steps.text:
After Unit 9 you can:

• Talk about hobbies with graag/liever/het liefst
• Make future plans with gaan + infinitive
• Make offers with 'Zal ik...?'
• Give reasons with omdat (verb at end!)
• Set conditions with als (verb at end!)
• Express purpose with om...te
• Talk about weather
• Celebrate Koningsdag

You've mastered the hardest word-order rule in Dutch.
That's a huge achievement.

steps.q: 'Ik ga morgen zwemmen' uses future with:

steps.s: ___ ik je helpen?
Ik blijf thuis omdat het {1}. Als de zon {2}, ga ik fietsen.
steps.hint: Because it RAINS. If the sun SHINES.

steps.s: Ik leer Nederlands ___ in Amsterdam te werken.

steps.q: 'Gaat wel' as an answer to 'Hoe gaat het?' means:
opts: Could be better, could be worse
steps.ans: Could be better, could be worse

steps.s: Ik zwem ___, maar ik wandel liever.
steps.hint: I like swimming, but I prefer walking

opts: It's the first day of spring
The Netherlands was founded that day

steps.s: Ik denk {1} hij ziek {2}.
steps.hint: I think THAT he sick IS.

steps.s: Wij ___ volgende week op vakantie.
steps.hint: We ARE GOING on holiday (wij = gaan)

steps.q: In 'Als het regent, blijf ik thuis' — 'blijf' comes before 'ik' because:
opts: The subordinate clause came first → inversion
steps.ans: The subordinate clause came first → inversion
